---
title: "Adding an AI Chat Assistant to a Static Website"
abstract: |
  Most personal websites are one-way streets: you publish, visitors read.
  What if they could *ask* instead? In this post I walk through adding a
  small AI assistant — powered by OpenAI and Cloudflare Workers — that lets
  anyone query my experience and résumé in plain English, with zero backend
  server to maintain.
date: 2026-03-08
date-modified: 2026-03-08
categories: [AI, Cloudflare, Quarto, Web]
draft: true
---

> **What you will build:** a floating chat widget on a static website that
> answers questions about your background using an LLM, backed by a
> serverless API you own and control.

## Why bother?

A PDF résumé is a frozen snapshot. Visitors have to skim it, guess what is
relevant to them, and hope they find the right section. A recruiter looking
for your Python experience and a professor curious about your teaching history
need very different things — but they both have to read the same document.

An AI assistant changes that dynamic. Instead of making the visitor do the
work, you let them ask in plain language: *"Does he have any experience with
time-series forecasting?"* or *"What level does he teach at?"* and get a
direct, personalised answer in seconds.

The goal of this post is to build exactly that, in a way that is:

- **Free (or nearly so)** — Cloudflare Workers has a generous free tier
  (100,000 requests/day); OpenAI costs fractions of a cent per conversation.
- **Private** — your OpenAI key never touches the browser; only your server
  ever sees it.
- **Maintenance-free** — no server to patch, no database, no uptime to worry
  about.
- **Portable** — the approach works with any static site generator (Quarto,
  Hugo, Jekyll, plain HTML…).

---

## The big picture

Before diving into code, it helps to understand the three moving parts and
how they talk to each other.

```
Visitor's browser
      │  1. types a question
      ▼
Chat widget (HTML/JS)
      │  2. POST { message } to Worker URL
      ▼
Cloudflare Worker  ──►  OpenAI API
      │                     │
      │  3. sends system     │  4. returns answer
      │     prompt + message │
      ◄─────────────────────-
      │  5. forwards answer
      ▼
Chat widget displays reply
```

**Step 1 – The widget** is a small piece of HTML and JavaScript injected into
every page of the site. It renders a floating chat button and panel, captures
the user's message, and sends it to the Worker.

**Step 2 & 3 – The Cloudflare Worker** is a tiny JavaScript function that
runs on Cloudflare's global network. It receives the message, prepends a
*system prompt* that describes your background in detail, and forwards
everything to OpenAI's API using your secret key.

**Step 4 & 5 – OpenAI** generates an answer and returns it. The Worker
passes it back to the browser, which displays it in the chat panel.

The key insight is that **the browser never talks to OpenAI directly**, which
means your API key stays secret on the server side, and you can add rate
limits or filters to the Worker later if you need them.

---

## Part 1 — The Cloudflare Worker

### What is a Cloudflare Worker?

Think of a Worker as a tiny script that lives "at the edge" — on servers
physically close to your visitors, all over the world. You write a JavaScript
function, deploy it once, and Cloudflare runs it automatically whenever
someone hits your chosen URL. There is no server to provision, no container
to manage, no uptime page to check.

The free plan allows **100,000 requests per day**, which is more than enough
for a personal website.

### Setting up Wrangler

Wrangler is the official Cloudflare command-line tool. You will use it to
log in, deploy your Worker, and store secrets.

```bash
# Install globally with npm (Node.js must be installed)
npm install -g wrangler

# Authenticate — this opens a browser window
wrangler login
```

If you do not have a Cloudflare account yet, the login flow will walk you
through creating a free one. No credit card required.

### The project structure

Create a `worker/` directory at the root of your site:

```
your-website/
├── worker/
│   ├── index.js       ← the Worker code
│   └── wrangler.toml  ← Cloudflare configuration
├── _partials/
│   └── chat-widget.html
└── … (rest of your site)
```

### `wrangler.toml` — telling Cloudflare about your Worker

```toml
name = "resume-chat"
main = "index.js"
compatibility_date = "2024-09-01"
```

- **`name`**: the subdomain your Worker will be deployed to
  (`resume-chat.<your-account>.workers.dev`).
- **`main`**: the entry-point file.
- **`compatibility_date`**: locks the Worker runtime to a stable feature set;
  use a recent date.

### `worker/index.js` — the Worker itself

Let's walk through the code section by section.

#### 1. The system prompt

The system prompt is the most important part of the whole setup. It is a
block of text that you prepend to *every* conversation, before the visitor's
message. It tells the LLM who it is, what it knows, and how to behave.

```js
const SYSTEM_PROMPT = `You are an AI assistant representing Guillaume Gilles.
Answer questions about his professional background, skills, and experience
based strictly on the information below. Be concise, friendly, and professional.
If asked about something not covered, say you don't have that information.

--- PROFILE ---
Guillaume Gilles is a former Banque de France financial analyst who now
channels a decade of risk assessment and teaching into cybersecurity…

--- EXPERIENCE ---
Regional Economist — Banque de France (2020 – Present)
…

--- EDUCATION ---
…

--- SKILLS ---
…`;
```

A few things to notice:

- **"based strictly on the information below"** — this instruction keeps the
  model from hallucinating or making up things that are not in your résumé.
- **"Be concise"** — without this, LLMs tend to produce very long answers.
- The content mirrors your résumé exactly — keep it updated whenever your
  situation changes.

#### 2. CORS headers

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security feature. When your
chat widget (hosted at `guillaumegilles.github.io`) tries to call your Worker
(hosted at `resume-chat.workers.dev`), the browser checks whether the Worker
explicitly allows that. If it does not, the browser blocks the request.

```js
const CORS_HEADERS = {
  "Access-Control-Allow-Origin": "*",
  "Access-Control-Allow-Methods": "POST, OPTIONS",
  "Access-Control-Allow-Headers": "Content-Type",
};
```

Setting `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` means any website can call your
Worker. This is fine for a public résumé assistant. If you wanted to restrict
it to your domain only, you would set the value to `https://yoursite.com`
instead.

#### 3. Handling the preflight `OPTIONS` request

Before every cross-origin `POST`, the browser automatically sends a quick
`OPTIONS` "preflight" request to ask the server: *"Are you OK with this?"*
Your Worker must respond to it correctly, or the real request never goes
through.

```js
export default {
  async fetch(request, env) {
    // Handle the browser's preflight check
    if (request.method === "OPTIONS") {
      return new Response(null, { headers: CORS_HEADERS });
    }
```

#### 4. Parsing the incoming message

```js
    let body;
    try {
      body = await request.json();
    } catch {
      return new Response("Invalid JSON", { status: 400 });
    }

    const { message, history = [] } = body;
```

The widget sends a JSON object with two fields:

- **`message`** — the visitor's current question.
- **`history`** — the last few messages of the conversation, so the LLM
  has context and can answer follow-up questions sensibly.

#### 5. Calling OpenAI

```js
    const messages = [
      { role: "system", content: SYSTEM_PROMPT },
      ...history.slice(-6),   // keep last 3 exchanges
      { role: "user", content: message },
    ];

    const openaiRes = await fetch(
      "https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions",
      {
        method: "POST",
        headers: {
          "Content-Type": "application/json",
          Authorization: `Bearer ${env.OPENAI_API_KEY}`,
        },
        body: JSON.stringify({
          model: "gpt-4o-mini",
          messages,
          max_tokens: 400,
          temperature: 0.4,
        }),
      }
    );
```

A few decisions worth explaining:

- **`gpt-4o-mini`** is fast and cheap — well suited to short Q&A. As of
  early 2026, it costs roughly $0.15 per million input tokens and $0.60 per
  million output tokens. A typical résumé conversation will use a few hundred
  tokens. The cost per visitor is a fraction of a cent.
- **`max_tokens: 400`** caps the answer length so the widget panel does not
  overflow with text.
- **`temperature: 0.4`** makes the model slightly less creative and more
  factual — desirable for a résumé assistant.
- **`env.OPENAI_API_KEY`** — the key is read from the Worker's secret store,
  not from the code. This is crucial: secrets stored this way never appear in
  your source code or repository.

#### 6. Returning the reply

```js
    const data = await openaiRes.json();
    const reply = data.choices[0].message.content;

    return new Response(JSON.stringify({ reply }), {
      headers: {
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        ...CORS_HEADERS,
      },
    });
  },
};
```

The Worker extracts the text reply from OpenAI's response and sends it back
to the browser as a simple `{ reply: "…" }` JSON object.

### Storing your API key securely

Never put secrets in code. Wrangler provides a dedicated secret store:

```bash
cd worker/
wrangler secret put OPENAI_API_KEY
# Wrangler will prompt you to paste the value — it is never logged
```

The value is encrypted and only accessible inside your Worker as
`env.OPENAI_API_KEY`. Not even Cloudflare's dashboard shows it in plain text
after you set it.

### Deploying

```bash
cd worker/
wrangler deploy
```

Wrangler prints something like:

```
Deployed resume-chat to
https://resume-chat.abc123.workers.dev
```

Copy that URL — you will need it in the next part.

You can test it immediately from the terminal:

```bash
curl -X POST https://resume-chat.abc123.workers.dev \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message": "What is his current job?"}'
# → {"reply":"Guillaume is currently a Regional Economist at the Banque de France…"}
```

---

## Part 2 — The chat widget

The widget is a self-contained HTML file that gets injected into every page
of the site. It has three parts: styles, the HTML structure, and the
JavaScript logic.

### The HTML structure

```html
<!-- Floating action button — always visible -->
<button id="chat-fab" title="Ask about my experience">💬</button>

<!-- The chat panel — hidden until the button is clicked -->
<div id="chat-panel">
  <div id="chat-header">
    <span>🤖 Ask about my experience</span>
    <button id="chat-close">✕</button>
  </div>
  <div id="chat-messages">
    <!-- Messages appear here dynamically -->
    <div class="chat-msg bot">
      Hi! Ask me anything about Guillaume's background… 👋
    </div>
  </div>
  <div id="chat-input-row">
    <input id="chat-input" type="text" placeholder="e.g. What languages does he speak?" />
    <button id="chat-send">Send</button>
  </div>
</div>
```

The floating button (`chat-fab`) is positioned in the bottom-right corner
using CSS `position: fixed`. Clicking it toggles the `.open` class on the
panel, which switches it from `display: none` to `display: flex`.

### The CSS

A few key choices:

```css
#chat-fab {
  position: fixed;
  bottom: 1.5rem;
  right: 1.5rem;
  z-index: 9999;          /* stays on top of everything */
  border-radius: 50%;     /* circular button */
  background: #4f46e5;    /* indigo — feel free to change */
}

#chat-panel {
  position: fixed;
  bottom: 5.5rem;         /* sits just above the button */
  right: 1.5rem;
  width: min(380px, calc(100vw - 2rem));  /* responsive */
  max-height: 520px;
  display: none;          /* hidden by default */
}
#chat-panel.open { display: flex; }
```

The `width: min(380px, calc(100vw - 2rem))` trick ensures the panel is
380 px wide on desktop but never wider than the screen on mobile, keeping
1 rem of margin on each side.

User messages and assistant replies are styled differently to make the
conversation easy to follow:

```css
.chat-msg.user {
  align-self: flex-end;        /* right-aligned */
  background: #4f46e5;         /* indigo bubble */
  color: #fff;
}
.chat-msg.bot {
  align-self: flex-start;      /* left-aligned */
  background: var(--bs-secondary-bg, #f1f3f5);
  color: var(--bs-body-color, #212529);
}
```

The `var(--bs-…)` CSS variables come from Bootstrap (which Quarto uses),
so the widget automatically adapts to light and dark themes without any
extra JavaScript.

### The JavaScript

```js
const WORKER_URL = "https://resume-chat.abc123.workers.dev";

let history = [];  // stores the conversation for context
let busy = false;  // prevents double-sending

async function sendMessage() {
  const text = input.value.trim();
  if (!text || busy) return;

  busy = true;
  send.disabled = true;
  input.value = "";

  appendMsg(text, "user");                        // show user bubble
  const thinking = appendMsg("Thinking…", "bot typing");  // show placeholder

  const res = await fetch(WORKER_URL, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify({ message: text, history }),
  });

  const data = await res.json();
  thinking.textContent = data.reply;             // replace placeholder
  thinking.className = "chat-msg bot";           // remove italic style

  // Store exchange in history for follow-up context
  history.push({ role: "user", content: text });
  history.push({ role: "assistant", content: data.reply });

  busy = false;
  send.disabled = false;
}
```

The `history` array is the mechanism for multi-turn conversation. Each
time a reply arrives, the exchange is appended to `history` and sent back
to the Worker with the next message. The Worker keeps only the last 6
entries (`history.slice(-6)`) to avoid sending very long payloads that would
cost more and slow things down.

---

## Part 3 — Integrating with Quarto

Quarto's `include-after-body` option lets you inject arbitrary HTML at the
end of every generated page, just before the closing `</body>` tag. This is
exactly what you need to add the widget site-wide without modifying every
individual page.

In `_quarto.yml`:

```yaml
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    theme:
      light: assets/light.scss
      dark: assets/dark.scss
    include-after-body: _partials/chat-widget.html   # ← add this line
```

That single line is all Quarto needs. After a `quarto render`, the widget
will appear on every page — blog posts, the about page, the teaching
section — everywhere.

---

## Putting it all together

Here is the complete deployment checklist:

```bash
# 1. Install Wrangler
npm install -g wrangler

# 2. Authenticate with Cloudflare (creates a free account if needed)
wrangler login

# 3. Deploy the Worker
cd worker/
wrangler deploy
# Note the URL printed: https://resume-chat.<subdomain>.workers.dev

# 4. Store your OpenAI API key
wrangler secret put OPENAI_API_KEY
# Paste your key when prompted

# 5. Update the widget with your Worker URL
# Open _partials/chat-widget.html and change:
#   const WORKER_URL = "https://resume-chat.YOUR-SUBDOMAIN.workers.dev";
# to your actual URL.

# 6. Rebuild the site
cd ..
quarto render

# 7. Publish
git add -A
git commit -m "Add AI résumé chat assistant"
git push
```

---

## What to customise

Once the basic setup works, here are the most common things you will want
to tweak:

**Change the accent colour** — search for `#4f46e5` in
`_partials/chat-widget.html` and replace it with your preferred hex colour.

**Update the résumé content** — open `worker/index.js` and edit the
`SYSTEM_PROMPT` string. Then run `wrangler deploy` again to push the
update. The website itself does not need to be rebuilt.

**Restrict which pages show the widget** — instead of setting
`include-after-body` globally in `_quarto.yml`, you can add it to the
front matter of individual pages:

```yaml
---
title: "About"
include-after-body: ../../_partials/chat-widget.html
---
```

**Tighten the CORS policy** — if you only want your own domain to be able
to call the Worker, change the header in `worker/index.js`:

```js
"Access-Control-Allow-Origin": "https://guillaumegilles.github.io",
```

**Swap the model** — replace `"gpt-4o-mini"` with `"gpt-4o"` for higher
quality answers (at a higher cost), or try another OpenAI model depending on
your needs.

---

## Closing thoughts

The whole implementation is about 130 lines of code spread across two files.
Despite its small footprint, it covers several important concepts that show
up in many real-world projects: serverless functions, CORS, secret management,
prompt engineering, and progressive enhancement (the site works perfectly for
visitors who never open the chat).

If you replicate this on your own site, feel free to open a
[GitHub issue](https://github.com/guillaumegilles/guillaumegilles.github.io)
or reach out on LinkedIn — I'd be happy to help you debug it.
